nativeRange.habitat: Riverside colonies in the central highlands of Sri Lanka, particularly near Kandy, where the plant grows in dense low groups along shaded stream margins

summary: The smallest of the Cryptocoryne genus and one of the few true foreground carpet crypts; a slow-growing Sri Lankan endemic with grassy, deep-green leaves prized for nano and Iwagumi-style aquascapes.
careNotes: Cryptocoryne parva is widely regarded as the smallest species in the genus Cryptocoryne, typically reaching only 3–8 cm in height with slender, deep-green strap-like leaves that grow in tidy rosettes. It is endemic to the central highlands of Sri Lanka (particularly around Kandy) and is one of the very few Cryptocoryne species suitable for use as a true foreground carpet plant. Growth is notably slow — slower than typical foreground carpeters like Monte Carlo or dwarf hairgrass — so a fully filled carpet can take 4–12 months. Because of this slow pace, plants are commonly spaced about 2–3 cm apart, often using multiple pots to accelerate coverage. CO2 injection is optional but consistently improves growth rate and colour. A nutrient-rich substrate (or inert substrate supplemented with root tabs every 2–3 months) is strongly preferred, as C. parva is a heavy root feeder. Like other Cryptocoryne species, C. parva is highly susceptible to 'crypt melt' during acclimation, particularly when transitioning from emersed nursery growth (most farm stock is grown emersed, meaning above the waterline) to a submerged home aquarium. Leaves may yellow and dissolve within days of planting; this is normal, and the rhizome and roots almost always survive — new submerged-form leaves typically regrow over the following weeks to about a month. Melted leaves are best left in place until they are fully dead, and the plant should not be uprooted during this period. The species is also sensitive to sudden swings in water chemistry, temperature, or substrate disturbance, all of which can trigger fresh melt episodes, so stable conditions matter more than hitting precise parameter targets. The plant should be set shallowly, with only the roots anchored in the substrate and the crown left above it.
breedingNotes: Propagates asexually by sending out thin white runners through the substrate, with daughter rosettes forming at intervals of a few centimetres. Once a parent rosette is well established (commonly after 2–3 months) it begins to spread; new plantlets can be carefully separated and replanted, or left in place to thicken the carpet. Sexual (flower-based) propagation is rare in aquarium conditions.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica species page (Cryptocoryne parva, product 049). Used for: smallest-Cryptocoryne descriptor (3–6 cm in their listing), medium lighting requirement (more light than most other crypts), slow growth, Sri Lanka origin, and the recommendation to space plants a few cm apart for cohesive foreground groups.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked Sri Lanka origin, Araceae family, foreground/midground placement, slow growth, low-light tolerance with improved growth under CO2 and quality soil, and the warning that drastic parameter changes trigger crypt melt. pH range listed as 6.0–7.5.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked carpeting foreground use, low-light viability with multi-pot planting for faster coverage, runner-based propagation, and the deep-green grass-like leaf morphology.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (Aquariumbreeder)
additional.notes: Cross-checked detailed parameters: height 3–8 cm, temperature 20–28°C, pH 5.5–7.5, gH 1–20, family Araceae / order Alismatales, native range central highlands of Sri Lanka near Kandy, propagation via runners up to ~20 cm from mother plant, and crypt-melt acclimation guidance (do not trim, plant may regrow from rhizome).

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked height 5–10 cm, temperature 20–29°C, pH 5–8 (optimal 6–7.5), kH 1–18 dKH, slow growth, easy-to-moderate care level, and crypt-melt acclimation (~1 month recovery).
